retrieved_docs.title: Intellectual Disability -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: According to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition (DSM-5), the diagnosis of intellectual disability requires deficits in intellectual function, deficits in adaptive function, and onset before the age of 22. The IQ test is widely used to assess the intellectual function of individuals. IQ test derives from Stanford-Binet Intelligence Scales, used for school placement in France. Lewis Terman adapted the test to measure general intelligence. Scores were reported as “mental age” divided by chronological age, multiplied by 100. The current version of the IQ test is standardized, and two standard deviations below the test taker’s group are calculated as an IQ of 70. An IQ of 70 or below suggests an intellectual disability diagnosis. Based on the IQ score, the severity grading appears below.

retrieved_docs.title: Intellectual Disability -- Introduction -- Intellectual Functioning
retrieved_docs.content: Intellectual functioning is commonly measured by the intelligence quotient (IQ), which represents a total score obtained from standardized tests (IQ tests) developed for evaluating human intelligence. IQ test score has a median of 100 and a standard deviation of 15. A score of 70 or below (two standard deviations below the median) indicates intellectual limitations.

retrieved_docs.title: Intellectual Disability -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: IQ 50 to 70: mild intellectual disability (85% of cases) IQ 35 to 50: moderate intellectual disability (10% of cases) IQ 20 to 35: severe intellectual disability (4% of cases) IQ below 20: Profound intellectual disability (1% of cases)

retrieved_docs.title: Intellectual Disability -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: However, it is no longer a standard to classify intellectual disability by IQ score alone. For instance, if an individual has an IQ below 70 but has a good adaptive function, the subject does not have an intellectual disability. On the other side, individuals with a normal or even higher than normal IQ may manifest severe deficits in adaptive functions and are, therefore, classified as having an intellectual disability. In turn, the current diagnosis of intellectual disability also considers a person’s adaptive function. [18] The Adaptive Behaviour Assessment System can measure adaptive function. [19] It encompasses the social and practical domains. Adaptive function measures ability in communication, social participation, and independent living.

retrieved_docs.title: Cognitive Development -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Intellectual disability is defined when there is a concern for intellectual and adaptive functioning. Usually, on standardized measures, this means a score less than two standard deviations below the mean, which is 100 for most measures. Standardized tests used to measure intellectual function include the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children (WISC), the Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence (WPPSI), and the Stanford-Binet test. One standardized test for adaptive functioning is the Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scale.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroblastoma -- Staging -- Subgroup Classification
retrieved_docs.content: However, COG classification of neuroblastoma has differed from the European SIOPEN and other cooperative groups. In 2004, the International Neuroblastoma Risk Group (INRG), a task force of pediatric neuroblastoma experts worldwide, met with the primary aim of developing a consensus approach to neuroblastoma risk stratification pre-treatment. [21] Using patient age, tumor stage, tumor grade, and differentiation, tumor histology, MYCN amplification, cytogenetic aberrations in 11q, and DNA ploidy, the group defined 16 pre-treatment groups broadly divisible into four prognostic subgroups based on five-year event-free survival (EFS): Very low risk: five-year event-free survival (EFS) greater than 85% Low risk: five-year EFS 75% to 85% Intermediate risk: five-year EFS 50% to 75% High risk: five-year EFS less than 50%

retrieved_docs.title: Cognitive Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Stages of Cognitive Development (Problem-Solving and Intelligence)
retrieved_docs.content: Two to five years: During this stage, the preschool years, magical and wishful thinking emerges; for example, the sun went home because it was tired. This ability may also give rise to apprehensions with fear of monsters, and having logical solutions may not be enough for reassurance. Perception will dominate over logic, and giving them an imaginary tool, like a monster spray, to help relieve that anxiety may be more helpful. Similarly, conservation and volume concept lacks, and what appears bigger or larger is more. For example, one cookie split into may equal two cookies.

retrieved_docs.title: Intellectual Disability -- Introduction -- Intellectual Functioning
retrieved_docs.content: Intellectual functioning is generally called intelligence and includes a wide range of mental activities, such as the ability of logical reasoning and practical intelligence (problem-solving), the ability to learn, verbal skills, and so on. It manifests and expresses itself through numerous sets of capabilities, behaviors, thoughts, and emotions. In other words, intellectual functioning is defined as the global ability that allows the individual to understand and interact with reality.

retrieved_docs.title: Klinefelter Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A wide range of intelligence quotients (IQs) has been reported. However, the mean full-scale IQ falls between 85 and 90, with an average reduction of approximately 15 points. [22] Verbal difficulties often stem from deficits in expressive language and auditory processing. [23] Behavioral features may include immaturity, insecurity, shyness, poor judgment, and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. Individuals with Klinefelter syndrome are at a higher risk of anxiety and depression, often related to body image concerns, interpersonal challenges, and infertility. [24] [25] [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Williams Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Intellectual disability in Williams syndrome is assessed using the Kaufman Brief Intelligence Test, Second Edition (KBIT-2). The assessment computes a composite intelligence quotient and verbal and nonverbal standard scores. [6] Studies utilizing KBIT-2 to measure intelligence quotient in children with Williams syndrome found that their intelligence quotient ranged from average to severe intellectual disability. [6] Children with Williams syndrome often struggle with visuospatial construction, which the KBIT-2 does not measure. [6] The Differential Ability Scale-II (DAS-II) Special Nonverbal Composite assessments and Wechsler intelligence quotient tests may be useful diagnostic tools to measure visuospatial construction. [6] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Delay -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Delay in development is generally determined with a child does not attain developmental milestones as compared to peers from the same population. Statistical terms are often used to classify the degree of delay into mild (functional age (FA) <33% below chronological age (CA), moderate (FA 34% to 66% of CA), and severe (FA <66% of CA). [3] [4] "Developmental delay" is a general descriptor of a broad phenotype that must then be specified by carefully determining one or more elements linked with the area of disrupted development. Developmental delay is not a diagnosis by itself rather a categorical, illustrative term used in the clinic. [5] Further, the terms often used to specify a developmental delay may vary by country or area of practice.  For example, the term "mental retardation is no longer in use with "learning disability " being used in the UK and Intellectual Disability being used in the US to define a group of individuals with a significant delay defined by "performance equal or greater than two standard deviations below the mean on age-appropriate standardized norm-referenced testing" (IQ or intelligent quotient testing). There are three types of developmental delay based on the number of domains involved: 1) Isolated developmental delay (involving single domain); 2. Multiple Developmental Delays — 2 or more domains or developmental lines affected; and, 3) Global developmental delay (GDD) —significant delay in most developmental domains. [1] [3] Other entities with abnormal developmental models are:

retrieved_docs.title: Mental Status Examination -- Function -- Cognition
retrieved_docs.content: Memory is subdivided into immediate recall, delayed recall, recent memory, and long-term memory. A clinician can assess 1 or all types of memory during evaluation. Immediate recall involves asking the patient to repeat a list of random words, numbers, or sentences to determine if a patient can register new information. Delayed recall requires the patient to repeat the exact words after a certain amount of time, typically 1 to 5 minutes, after performing another task, which prevents the patient from practicing the answer. If a patient does not have delayed recall, they may be able to remember the information if given hints. In this case, a patient's delayed recall is not intact, but prompted recall is. [3] Recent memory involves evaluating the patient's ability to recall recent events, which can be done by asking about their recent activities or the history of their present illness. Long-term memory assesses a patient's memory to recall events from their distant past. Examples are asking patients about when they had a child, what high school they attended, their childhood home, or their wedding. If a patient has impaired responses to recall testing or memory, this may indicate a neurocognitive disorder that requires further screening with one of the assessments mentioned at the beginning of this section. If a patient presents specifically with a cognitive complaint, the extensive evaluation should include assessing activities of daily living, a focused neurological examination, and validated instruments mentioned above, including NPI-Q, Functional Activities Questionnaire, General Practitioner Assessment of Cognition, GDS, IQCODE, or Memory Impairment Screen. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Cognitive
retrieved_docs.content: Mild to moderate intellectual disability affects most patients with Kabuki syndrome. [5] This is partly due to a deficiency in postnatal neurogenesis as a direct result of the KMT2D mutation, suggesting a neural basis for cognitive and intellectual impairment. [4] Average intelligence quotient (IQ) scores with KMT2D pathogenic variants have been reported to be in the high 50s to high 60s, although individuals with a more severe disability may be nonverbal. Severe intellectual disabilities may be found in patients with truncating variants in the first half of KMT2D or those with a whole deletion of KMT2D . Rare pathogenic variants of KMT2D and KDM6A have reported IQ levels above 70. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Learning Disability -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In addition, comprehensive neuropsychological testing can further guide the clinician toward the correct diagnosis. Commonly administered tests include: Woodcock-Johnson-III and Wide Range Achievement Test to evaluate academic achievement; the Adaptive Behavior Assessment System-II and the Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scale-II to assess adaptive behavior; the Conners Rating Scale, ADHD Rating Scale-IV (formerly DuPaul scale) and NICHQ Vanderbilt Parent and Teacher Assessment Scales to evaluate for attention and hyperactive disorders; the Wechsler Intelligence Scales for Children—Fourth Edition (WISC-IV) for evaluation of general cognition; the Achenbach Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) for assessment of general behavior; and the Clinical Evaluation of Language Fundamentals (4th Ed.)

retrieved_docs.title: Cognitive Assessment -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The cognitive assessment is useful to test for cognitive impairment—a deficiency in knowledge, thought process, or judgment. Psychiatrists often perform cognitive testing during the Mental Status Exam. However, when cognitive impairment is suspected, the cognitive assessment can obtain a more detailed analysis by surveying the neuropsychological domains. This detailed investigation of cognition can diagnose major cognitive impairment (ie, dementia) and mild cognitive impairment, evaluate traumatic brain injuries, help determine decision-making capacity, and survey intellectual dysfunction. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Asperger Syndrome -- Introduction -- DSM Classification
retrieved_docs.content: Addition of three levels of severity for ASD. These severity levels help provide a more comprehensive understanding of the individual's functional impairments and support needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: The network links gestational timing, birth outcomes, and perinatal pathology through multiple classification systems. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is recorded as a **liveborn** outcome; liveborn is a subtype of **Duration of gestation**, which is coded within the **International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM)**. Within ICD‑10‑AM, **Duration of gestation** has the child concept **اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ** (“respiratory disorder”). The condition **Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation** (Arabic: تقييد النمو الجنيني and ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}) both classifies and is classified by **liveborn**, designating infants with these characteristics as a specific subgroup of liveborn births. This condition serves as the parent category for **Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96)**, which in turn includes two child entities:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 699
retrieved_docs.content: the definition of liveborn status, and it is situated within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), providing a coding framework for these concepts. Collectively, these hierarchical and classificatory relationships demonstrate how gestational age, birth weight, and fetal growth parameters intersect with broader perinatal disease categories, supporting clinical coding, epidemiologic surveillance, and comprehensive patient care for newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 263
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concepts described are tightly linked through hierarchical and associative relationships that clarify how gestational timing, perinatal disorders, and reproductive health services intersect. The specific gestational interval “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is connected to the outcome category “liveborn,” indicating that a liveborn infant can result from pregnancies reaching this stage. Conversely, the broader classification “Disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight” lists “liveborn” as its parent, positioning liveborn as a more general category that encompasses such disorders. “Duration of gestation” is defined as an inverse‑isa of “liveborn,” meaning that the liveborn status is a particular instance within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. Encounters with health services for reproductive reasons (ICD‑10 Z30‑Z39) also cite “liveborn” as a parent, reflecting that liveborn infants are a key focus of reproductive health care encounters. The

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1983
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is classified within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), indicating that gestational length is a recognized medical category in this coding system. Within this framework, the specific gestational interval of “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is linked to the outcome of a “liveborn” infant, reflecting that infants born after completing 29 to 30 weeks of pregnancy can be alive at birth. Accordingly, the “liveborn” outcome is treated as a distinct subclass or child concept under the broader umbrella of gestational duration, illustrating how gestational age ranges and birth outcomes are organized together in clinical classification and patient‑care documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 388
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period described as “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is classified under the Arabic term خديج (preterm birth) and is linked to a liveborn outcome. Preterm births (خديج) are frequently caused by متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). The concept of Duration of gestation is a key attribute of liveborn infants and is organized within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM). Within this classification system, respiratory disorders (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ) are listed as a child category, reflecting the close relationship between gestational age, preterm status, underlying pulmonary immaturity, and respiratory complications. Recognizing these interconnections and their coding in ICD‑10‑AM supports accurate diagnosis, documentation, and clinical management of infants born at 29–30 weeks gestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of a liveborn infant encompasses a range of perinatal classifications that are linked through gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth status. A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a specific child category of liveborn, indicating that infants born alive at this stage are included within the broader liveborn group. The duration of gestation has an inverse‑isa relationship with liveborn, reflecting that shorter gestational periods are associated with distinct clinical classifications of liveborn infants. One major classification of liveborn infants is short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This category both classifies liveborn and is self‑referential, emphasizing its central role in describing infants born with reduced gestational length, low birth weight, or impaired growth. The disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight is positioned as a parent of liveborn, indicating that this disorder underlies the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: the coding system “International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification” (ICD‑10‑AM) through an inverse‑ISA relationship, and this classification system contains the Arabic child entity “اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ.” Collectively, the network shows that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serve as foundational categories that give rise to specific fetal‑growth disorders, perinatal respiratory and cardiovascular conditions—including respiratory distress syndrome—and are consistently represented in international diagnostic coding systems across multiple languages, supporting comprehensive assessment, accurate diagnosis, and standardized documentation of perinatal health issues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1134
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) represent a broad ICD‑10 chapter that encompasses a variety of neonatal disorders. Within this chapter, respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29) are classified as a child subgroup, highlighting the importance of recognizing and managing lung and heart problems that arise immediately after birth. Another distinct subgroup is Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation, which is both a specific diagnostic category and a self‑defining classification for infants born prematurely, with low birth weight, or with impaired intrauterine growth. This condition is situated under the umbrella of Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96), reflecting its role as a specific perinatal disorder within the larger classification system. Understanding these hierarchical relationships aids clinicians in accurate coding, targeted assessment, and appropriate therapeutic planning for newborns with perinatal health issues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: reinforce the connection between neonatal respiratory compromise, extreme prematurity, and perinatal infection. This integrated hierarchy supports comprehensive patient care by aligning gestational age, birth weight, fetal growth, and respiratory complications with their appropriate diagnostic categories across ICD‑10‑AM, WHO perinatal codes, and multilingual clinical terminologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2503
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is organized within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), which also encompasses conditions such as اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder). Within this classification framework, “liveborn” is regarded as a specific outcome associated with gestational duration; in particular, a gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed can result in a liveborn infant. This relationship positions liveborn as a concrete instance of the broader category of gestational duration outcomes, illustrating how gestational age, birth status, and disease classification are interconnected in medical documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the model demonstrates that gestational age, birth weight, and fetal growth metrics intersect with diagnostic classifications, perinatal disease categories, and health‑service utilization, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, classifying, and managing conditions that arise around the time of birth.
